title: One small thing at a time
blurb: Climbing out of depression not with a leap, but with tiny steps.
lead: A story about depression's heaviness — and how the smallest actions slowly rebuilt a life.

blocks.text: Depression didn't feel like sadness. It felt like fog, and weight, and a voice insisting that nothing mattered and nothing would change. Getting out of bed was a mountain. Showering was a mountain. Everything was a mountain.
Advice to 'just exercise' or 'think positive' felt cruel — impossible from inside the fog.

What eventually helped was almost embarrassingly small. Not 'get your life together', but 'sit up'. Then 'put your feet on the floor'. Then 'stand outside for two minutes'. A therapist called it behavioural activation. It felt like cheating, doing things this small.
But the tiny things stacked up. Two minutes outside became a short walk. A short walk became a reason to get dressed. The fog didn't lift all at once, but light started getting through.
I couldn't think my way out. I had to act my way out — one ridiculously small step at a time.
This is an illustrative composite — a realistic story drawn from common experiences, not a real named individual. It's here to remind you that change is possible.
